content: Which one of the following CPU scheduling algorithms cannot be preemptive?
explanation:
The correct answer is Option B: First Come First Serve (FCFS) Scheduling.

Explanation:

A preemptive scheduling algorithm is one in which the CPU can be taken away from a running process before it finishes.

Now check each option:

  • SRTF (Shortest Remaining Time First)

    This is a preemptive version of shortest job scheduling.

    If a new process arrives with less remaining burst time, the current process is stopped.

  • FCFS (First Come First Serve)

    In this method, the process that comes first gets the CPU first and keeps it until completion.

    So it is non-preemptive.

  • Round Robin Scheduling

    This is preemptive because each process gets CPU for a fixed time quantum.

  • Priority Scheduling

    This can be preemptive or non-preemptive, depending on the system.

Final Answer:

$$\boxed{\text{FCFS Scheduling}}$$

content:
Processes $P 1, P 2, P 3, P 4$ arrive in that order at times $0,1,2$, and 8 milliseconds respectively, and have execution times of $10,13,6$, and 9 milliseconds respectively. Shortest Remaining Time First (SRTF) algorithm is used as the CPU scheduling policy. Ignore context switching times.
Which ONE of the following correctly gives the average turnaround time of the four processes in milliseconds?
explanation:
Process A.T. B.T. C.T. T.A.T.
$P_1$ 0 10 16 16
$P_2$ 1 13 38 37
$P_3$ 2 6 8 6
$P_4$ 8 9 25 17

Gantt Chart:

Average Turn Around Time $=\frac{16+37+6+17}{4}=\frac{76}{4}=19$

content:
Suppose in a multiprogramming environment, the following C program segment is executed. A process goes into I/O queue whenever an I/O related operation is performed. Assume that there will always be a context switch whenever a process requests for an I/O, and also whenever the process returns from an I/O. The number of times the process will enter the ready queue during its lifetime (not counting the time the process enters the ready queue when it is run initially) is _________ . (Answer in integer)


int main( )
{
    int x = 0,i=0;
    scanf("%d", &x);
    for(i=0; i<20;i++)
    {
        x=x+20;
        printf("%d\n", x);
    }
    return 0;
}
explanation:
To understand how many times the process enters the ready queue after it first starts, let's analyze the program's flow:

Program Execution Steps:

Program Start and Initial Entry to Ready Queue:

The process starts execution and enters the ready queue initially. This entry is not counted for this problem.

User Input with scanf:

The statement scanf("%d", &x); takes input from the user.

The process performs an I/O operation, moves to the I/O queue, and a context switch occurs.

After completing the I/O, the process returns to the ready queue. 1 entry to the ready queue occurs here.

For Loop Execution (20 iterations):

Within the loop, printf("%d\n", x); is executed in each iteration.

Each printf causes an I/O operation, moving the process to the I/O queue followed by a return to the ready queue after I/O completes.

Since there are 20 iterations, the process enters the ready queue 20 additional times due to the 20 printf statements.

Calculating Total Entries to the Ready Queue:

From scanf: 1 entry

From printf (20 iterations): 20 entries

Therefore, the total number of times the process enters the ready queue during its lifetime, excluding the initial entry, is:

$ 1 + 20 = 21 $

In conclusion, the process will enter the ready queue 21 times in total after the initial start.

content: Consider a process P running on a CPU. Which one or more of the following events will always trigger a context switch by the OS that results in process P moving to a non-running state (e.g., ready, blocked)?

options.content: P makes a blocking system call to read a block of data from the disk
P tries to access a page that is in the swap space, triggering a page fault
An interrupt is raised by the disk to deliver data requested by some other process
A timer interrupt is raised by the hardware

explanation:
To determine which events will always trigger a context switch that results in process P moving to a non-running state, let's analyze each option in detail:

Option A:

When process P makes a blocking system call to read a block of data from the disk, the process will enter a blocked state until the data is read from the disk. This means a context switch will occur to allow another process to use the CPU while P is waiting for the data. Therefore, Option A always results in process P moving to a non-running state.

Option B:

If process P tries to access a page that is in the swap space, this will trigger a page fault. Handling a page fault requires the OS to read the required page from the swap space into physical memory. During this time, process P cannot continue executing and must be moved to a blocked state, necessitating a context switch. Thus, Option B always results in process P moving to a non-running state.

Option C:

When an interrupt is raised by the disk to deliver data requested by some other process, the interrupt service routine (ISR) will be executed by the CPU. However, the interrupt itself does not necessarily cause process P to move to a non-running state. The interrupt may be processed, and process P could continue running if it is not directly affected. Therefore, Option C does not always result in process P moving to a non-running state.

Option D:

A timer interrupt is raised by the hardware to maintain the system's time clock or for preemptive multitasking purposes. In a preemptive multitasking system, a timer interrupt can lead to a context switch if the OS decides to schedule another process. However, this does not mean process P will always move to a non-running state—process P could remain in the ready state if it still has time left in its time slice. Thus, Option D does not always result in process P moving to a non-running state.

In conclusion, the events that will always trigger a context switch by the OS and result in process P moving to a non-running state are:

Answer: Option A and Option B

content: Which of the following process state transitions is/are NOT possible?
explanation:
In the context of process state transitions within an operating system, certain transitions are typical and expected, while others are not. Let's analyze each of the given options:

Option A: Running to Ready
This transition occurs when a running process is interrupted by the scheduler to allocate CPU time to another process. It is a common and valid transition, known as preemption.

Option B: Waiting to Running
This transition is not typically possible without passing through the Ready state first. A process in the Waiting (or Blocked) state must first be moved to the Ready state once the condition it was waiting for is met (e.g., I/O completion). Only then can it be scheduled to Run.

Option C: Ready to Waiting
This transition is not possible. A process in the Ready state is waiting for CPU time and should only move to the Running state. A move from Ready to Waiting would imply the process needs to wait for an event without ever having run, which defies the standard state transition logic.

Option D: Running to Terminated
This transition occurs when a process completes its execution and terminates. This is a standard and valid transition.

Based on this analysis, the transitions that are NOT possible are:

Option B: Waiting to Running
Option C: Ready to Waiting

content: Which one or more of the following need to be saved on a context switch from one thread (T1) of a process to another thread (T2) of the same process?
explanation:
In a context switch between threads of the same process, certain aspects of the thread's state need to be preserved so that execution can resume correctly. Let's analyze each option to determine which ones are required to be saved:

Option A: Page table base register

The page table base register is not typically saved in a context switch between threads of the same process because threads share the same address space within a process. The page table base register is crucial in process switches, where different processes have their own address spaces, but for a context switch within the same process, this is unnecessary.

Option B: Stack pointer

The stack pointer is essential to be saved because each thread has its own stack. The stack pointer points to the current location in the stack that is being used by the thread. To ensure that T1’s context is preserved and T2 can resume correctly, the stack pointer must be saved and restored during a context switch.

Option C: Program counter

The program counter (also known as the instruction pointer) must also be saved. It holds the address of the next instruction to be executed. Without saving the program counter, the thread cannot resume execution from the point it was interrupted, making it essential for context switching between threads.

Option D: General purpose registers

General purpose registers hold intermediate data and are critical for the execution of a thread. These registers need to be saved so that the thread can resume its execution without data corruption or loss. Thus, saving the state of general purpose registers is necessary during a context switch.

In summary, the key components that need to be saved on a context switch from one thread to another within the same process are:

$$ \text{Option B: Stack pointer} $$

$$ \text{Option C: Program counter} $$

$$ \text{Option D: General purpose registers} $$

content: Which one or more of the following CPU scheduling algorithms can potentially cause starvation?
explanation:
Starvation in the context of CPU scheduling refers to a scenario where a process is perpetually delayed from accessing the CPU due to the continuous arrival of other processes that take precedence over it. Let's consider each of the given CPU scheduling algorithms to determine which can potentially cause starvation:

Option A: First-in First-out (FIFO)

First-in, First-out, also known as FIFO or FCFS (First-Come, First-Served), is the simplest type of CPU scheduling algorithm that schedules processes in the order they arrive in the ready queue. Since each process is given the CPU in the exact order they arrive without any other consideration, there is no case for starvation; every process will be executed in due time. However, this can lead to poor performance for time-critical processes if they get stuck behind long, non-urgent tasks.

For FIFO, in case of infinite loop like
while (1);
then it cause starvation so until there's special case of a task running forever there'll be no starvation. But this scenario is typically addressed through system design rather than scheduling.

Option B: Round Robin (RR)

Round Robin scheduling is designed to prevent starvation by allocating each process a fixed time slot or quantum. The CPU scheduler rotates through the ready queue, assigning the CPU to each process for a time interval not exceeding the predefined quantum. When a process's time quantum expires, it is placed at the end of the queue, if it still has work to do. This cycling ensures that no process is starved of CPU time, though individual processes may experience longer wait times under heavy load.

Option C: Priority Scheduling

Priority scheduling assigns each process a priority, and the CPU is allocated to the process with the highest priority (smallest integer = highest priority). If two processes have the same priority, they are typically scheduled according to FIFO rules. This can lead to starvation, as low-priority processes may never get the CPU if there are enough high-priority processes continuously entering the ready queue. Therefore, priority scheduling can potentially cause starvation unless mechanisms like priority aging are implemented to eventually increase the priority of waiting processes.

Option D: Shortest Job First (SJF)

Shortest Job First, or SJF, is a non-preemptive scheduling strategy that selects the process with the smallest estimated running time to execute next. This algorithm can cause starvation for processes with longer estimated run times if shorter processes keep arriving. Since the CPU always prefers the jobs estimated to finish quickest, longer processes could be delayed indefinitely.

In conclusion, both Option C (Priority Scheduling) and Option D (Shortest Job First) can potentially cause starvation, as they prioritize certain processes in a way that can indefinitely postpone the execution of others.

content: Which of the following statement(s) is/are correct in the context of CPU scheduling?

options.content: Implementing preemptive scheduling needs hardware support.
Round-robin policy can be used even when the CPU time required by each of the processes is not known apriori.
The goal is to only maximize CPU utilization and minimize throughput.

explanation:
To determine which statements are correct in the context of CPU scheduling, let's analyze each option individually.

Option A:

Implementing preemptive scheduling needs hardware support.

Analysis:

Preemptive Scheduling:

In preemptive scheduling, the operating system can interrupt a currently running process to assign the CPU to another process.

This requires the ability to forcefully regain control from a running process.

Hardware Support:

Timer Interrupts: Hardware timers generate interrupts at regular intervals.

The operating system uses these interrupts to preempt the running process.

Context Switching: The CPU must support context switching, saving the state of the current process and loading the state of another.

Conclusion:

Implementing preemptive scheduling requires hardware support in the form of timer interrupts and context-switching capabilities.

Option A is correct.

Option B:

Turnaround time includes waiting time.

Analysis:

Turnaround Time:

Defined as the total time taken from the submission of a process to its completion.

It includes all periods of waiting, execution, and I/O operations.

Components of Turnaround Time:

Waiting Time: Time the process spends in the ready queue waiting for CPU allocation.

Execution Time: Time during which the process is executing on the CPU.

I/O Time: Time spent waiting for or performing I/O operations.

Conclusion:

Waiting time is a part of turnaround time, along with execution and I/O times.

Option B is correct.

Option C:

Round-robin policy can be used even when the CPU time required by each of the processes is not known apriori.

Analysis:

Round-Robin Scheduling:

Each process is assigned a fixed time slice (quantum) in a cyclic order.

Processes are scheduled without requiring knowledge of their total CPU burst time.

CPU Time Requirement:

Round-robin scheduling does not need to know the total CPU time required by each process.

It handles processes dynamically, making it suitable when CPU times are unknown.

Conclusion:

Round-robin scheduling can be effectively used without prior knowledge of CPU time requirements.

Option C is correct.

Option D:

The goal is to only maximize CPU utilization and minimize throughput.

Analysis:

CPU Scheduling Goals:

Maximize CPU Utilization: Keep the CPU as busy as possible.

Maximize Throughput: Increase the number of processes completed per unit time.

Minimize Turnaround Time: Reduce the total time taken for a process to complete.

Minimize Waiting Time: Reduce the time processes spend waiting in the ready queue.

Minimize Response Time: Quick response for interactive users.

Statement Evaluation:

The goal is to maximize CPU utilization, which is correct.

However, the statement says "minimize throughput," which implies reducing the number of processes completed per unit time.

Minimizing throughput is not a goal; the goal is to maximize throughput.

Conclusion:

The statement is incorrect because it suggests minimizing throughput, which is contrary to scheduling objectives.

Option D is incorrect.

Final Answer:

Correct Statements: Options A, B, and C are correct.

Incorrect Statement: Option D is incorrect.

Answer: Options A, B, and C are correct.

content: Which of the following standard C library functions will always invoke a system call when executed from a single-threaded process in a UNIX/Linux operating system?
explanation:
Option A: strlen

Functionality: The strlen function calculates the length of a null-terminated string by iterating over each character until it encounters the null terminator '\0'.

System Calls: strlen operates entirely in user space. It does not perform any I/O operations or interact with the kernel.

Conclusion: strlen does not invoke any system calls.

Option B: malloc

Functionality: The malloc function allocates a specified amount of memory from the heap for use by the program.

System Calls:

Sometimes Invokes System Calls: When the heap requires expansion, malloc may internally invoke system calls like sbrk or mmap to request more memory from the operating system.

Not Always: However, if sufficient memory is already available in the heap (from previous allocations or free operations), malloc can allocate memory without invoking any system calls.

Conclusion: malloc does not always invoke a system call.

Option C: exit

Functionality: The exit function terminates the calling process and returns control to the operating system. It performs cleanup tasks like flushing I/O buffers and calling functions registered with atexit.

System Calls:

Always Invokes System Calls: Ultimately, exit needs to terminate the process, which requires a system call to inform the kernel. It typically invokes the _exit or exit_group system call to terminate the process.

Conclusion: exit always invokes a system call.

Option D: sleep

Functionality: The sleep function suspends the execution of the calling thread for a specified number of seconds.

System Calls:

Always Invokes System Calls: To suspend execution, sleep must interact with the kernel to block the process or thread. It typically uses system calls like nanosleep, clock_nanosleep, or sleep.

Conclusion: sleep always invokes a system call.

Final Answer

Functions that always invoke a system call:

Option C: exit

Option D: sleep

Functions that do not always invoke a system call:

Option A: strlen (never invokes a system call)

Option B: malloc (may invoke a system call, but not always)

Answer: Options C and D

content:
Consider the following statements about process state transitions for a system using preemptive scheduling.

I. A running process can move to ready state.

II. A ready process can move to ready state.

III. A blocked process can move to running state.

IV. A blocked process can move to ready state.

Which of the above statements are TRUE?
explanation:
Given statements :

I. A running process can move to ready state.

II. A ready process can move to running state.

III. A blocked process can move to running state.

IV. A blocked process can move to ready state.

A blocked process cannot go to running state directly. Except (III), every option is viable.

Hence, the correct option is (C).

When the value of 'i' is even then fork() statement is executed.

(1) when i = 0, then fork() will executed.

(2) when i = 2, then fork() will executed.

(3) when i = 4, then fork() will executed.

(4) when i = 6, then fork() will executed.

(5) when i = 8, then fork() will executed.

So total 5 times fork() will executed.

We know, n - fork statements will create 2n - 1 childs.

$$ \therefore $$ 5 fork statements will create 25 - 1 = 31 childs.

content:
A Computer handles several interrupt sources of which the following are relevant for this question:

$$ * \,\,\,$$ Interrupt from $$CPU$$ temperature sensor (raises interrupt if $$CPU$$ temperature is too high)

$$ * \,\,\,$$ Interrupt from Mouse (raises interrupt if the mouse is moved or a button is pressed)

$$ * \,\,\,$$ Interrupt from keyboard (raises interrupt when a key is pressed or release)

$$ * \,\,\,$$ Interrupt from Hard Disk (raises interrupt when a disk read is completed)>/p>

Which one these will be handled at the HIGHEST priority?

A thread is usually defined as a ''light weight process'' because an operating system $$(OS)$$ maintains smaller data structures for a thread than for a process. In relation to this, which of the following is TRUE?

options.content: On per-thread basis, the $$OS$$ maintains only $$CPU$$ register state.
The $$OS$$ does not maintain a separate stack for each thread
On per-thread basis, the $$OS$$ does not maintain virtual memory state.
On per-thread basis, the $$OS$$ maintains only scheduling and accounting

explanation: Operating system mantains a separate program counter,stack and CPU register set for a thread,so options A , B and D will be false. All the threads within a process/task share the same virtual memory space. Hence the OS does not maintain virtual memory space for each individual threads.

content:
Let the time taken to switch between user and kernel modes of execution be $${t_1}$$ while the time taken to switch between two processes be $${t_2}$$.

Which of the following is TRUE?

options.content: Nothing can be said about the relation between $${t_1}$$ and $${t_2}.$$

explanation: Since the kernel is what facilitates switching from one process to the other, you need to switch from user mode to kernel mode for the scheduler (which is part of the kernel) to take control, then, after it makes its decisions, you have a switch from kernel to user mode in the new process. So switching process to process likely takes at least twice as long as user to kernel, at least in the general case.

content:
Which of the following statements are true?
$${\rm I}.$$ Shortest remaining time first scheduling may cause starvation
$${\rm II}.$$ Preemptive scheduling may cause starvation
$${\rm III}.$$ Round robin is better than $$FCFS$$ in terms of response time
A CPU generally handles an interrupt by executing an interrupt service routine

options.content: As soon as an interrupt is raised
By checking the interrupt register at the end of fetch cycle.
By checking the interrupt register after finishing the execution of the current instruction.
By checking the interrupt register at fixed time intervals.

Group-1
(P) Gang Scheduling

Consider the following statements about user level threads and kernel level threads.

Which one of the following statements is FALSE?

options.content: Context switch time is longer for kernel level threads than for user level threads.
User level threads do not need any hardware support.
Related kernel level threads can be scheduled on different processors in a multi-processor system.
Blocking one kernel level thread blocks all related threads.

content: Consider three $$CPU$$-intensive process, which require $$10,20$$ and $$30$$ time units and arrive at times $$0,2$$ and $$6$$ respectively. How many context switches are needed if the operating system implements a shortest remaining time first scheduling algorithm? Do not count the context switches at time zero and at the end.

Consider the following statements with respect to user-level threads and kernel-supported threads.
i) Context switch is faster with kernel- supported threads
ii) For user-level threads, a system call can block the entire process
iii) Kernel-supported threads can be scheduled independently
iv) Use-level threads are transparent to the kernel

Which of the above statements are true?
explanation:
Case (i): This is False.
As in kernal level threads to go from one thread to other thread we need to call Operating System but in user-level-threads without calling the OS we can context switch from one thread to another.

Case (ii): This is True.
As in user-level-threads threads are created by the user, so Operating System's Kernal has no idea about how many threads are present inside a process/task.That is why when one thread get blocked entire process/task will be blocked by the OS.

Case (iii): This is True.
As kernal supported threads are created by kernal itself,so kernal knows how many threads are present inside a process/task.That is why kernal can schedule each thread independently.

Case (iv): This is True.
As in user-level-threads threads are created by the user, so Operating System's kernal has no idea about how many threads are present inside a process/task.So we can say user-level-threads are transparent to the OS kernal.

content: Which of the following scheduling algorithms is non-preemptive?
explanation: Non-preemptive means untill a job gets finished the job is not going to removed from the CPU to execute a new job. FIFO/FCFS is non-preemptive others are preemptive.

content: A processor needs software interrupt to

options.content: Test the interrupt system of the processor
Obtain system services which need execution of privileged instructions

explanation: Privileged instruction means an instruction which is under the control of Operating System. Software interrupt let processor access those privilege instructions by changing user mode to kernal mode. That is how a processor access privileged instructions.

content: Consider a set of $$n$$ tasks with known runtimes $${r_1},{r_2},.....\,{r_n}\,\,$$ to be run on a uniprocessor machine. Which of the following processor scheduling algorithms will result in the maximum throughput?.
explanation: Throughput means no of processes completed per unit time. As in Shortest-job-first algorithm shorter jobs are executing earlier then no of jobs completed pre unit time will be maximum. So in Shortest-job-first algorithm throughput will be maximum.

content: A CPU has two modes-privileged and non-privileged. In order to change the mode from privileged to non-privileged

options.content: A privileged instruction (which does not generate an interrupt) is needed.
A non-privileged instruction (which does not generate an interrupt) is needed.

content: System calls are usually invoked by using:
explanation: When a program in user mode requires access to RAM or a hardware resource, it must switch to kernel mode to provide access to that resource. This is done via something called a system call and switching between user mode to kernel mode is done through software interrupt.

content: Which of the following is an example of spooled device?

options.content: The terminal used to enter the input data for the C program being executed.
An output device used to print the output of a number of jobs.
The secondary memory device in a virtual storage system.
The swapping area on a disk used by the swapper.

content: Consider $$n$$ processes sharing the $$CPU$$ in a round-robin fashion. Assuming that each process switch takes $$s$$ seconds, what must be the quantum size $$q$$ such that the overhead resulting from process switching is minimized but, at the same time each process is guaranteed to get its turn at the $$CPU$$ at least every $$t$$ seconds?
explanation: For every process next turn we will get after (n - 1) process execution and between that n context switches occurs. Time taken for executing (n - 1) process is [(n - 1)q] and for n context switches time takes = nq. According to question we can write-

content: The process state transition diagram in Figure is representative of

options.content: an operating system with a preemptive scheduler
an operating system with a non-preemptive scheduler

explanation: Here a process can go from running state to ready state. So it is always preemptive.

content: Which of the following is an example of spooled device?

options.content: A line printer used to print the output of a number of jobs.
A terminal used to enter input data to a running program.
A secondary storage device in a virtual memory system.

explanation:
SPOOL means simultaneous peripheral operations on-line, spooling refers to putting jobs in a buffer, a special area in memory or on a disk where a device can access them when it is ready. Spooling is useful because devices access data at different rates.

The most common spooling application is print spooling. Printers have a limited amount of memory, often times much smaller than the size of a document that you are wanting to print. Printer spooling allows you to send large documents, or multiple documents, to a printer and not have to wait for it to finish printing before continuing on to your next task.

content: Which scheduling policy is most suitable for a time-shared operating systems?
explanation: Round Robin is a pre-emptive scheduler, which is designed especially for time sharing system. In Round Robin, each process is given a time slot to run. If the process does not finish in that time slot, then it will put in a queue and new process will enter into the running state.

content:
Consider the following program snippet. Assume that the program compiles and runs successfully. Further, assume that the fork( ) system call is always successful in creating a process.

int main( ) {

int i;

for (i = 0; i < 3; i++){

    if (fork() == 0){

        continue;

    }

    break;

}

printf("Hello!");

return 0;

}

```

The total number of times that the printf statement gets executed is $\_\_\_\_$ . (answer in integer)
explanation:
Execution Trace:

At the start, there is only one process, say $P_0$.

1. First iteration: $i=0$

Process $P_0$ executes fork().

The child process gets value $0$ from fork(), so the condition fork() == 0 becomes true. Therefore, the child executes continue and goes to the next iteration.

The parent process gets a non-zero value from fork(), so the condition is false. Therefore, it does not go inside the if block and executes break.

So, parent $P_0$ comes out of the loop and executes printf("Hello!") once.

The child from this step, call it $C_1$, moves to the next iteration.

2. Second iteration: $i=1$

Now only process $C_1$ is inside the loop.

Process $C_1$ again executes fork().

Its child gets $0$, so it executes continue and goes to the next iteration.

Its parent gets a non-zero value, so it executes break and comes out of the loop.

Thus, process $C_1$ executes printf("Hello!") once.

The new child from this step, call it $C_2$, moves to the next iteration.

3. Third iteration: $i=2$

Now only process $C_2$ is inside the loop.

Process $C_2$ executes fork().

Its child gets $0$, so it executes continue. After this, the loop variable becomes $i=3$, so the loop condition fails and the child comes out of the loop normally.

This child now executes printf("Hello!") once.

The parent process $C_2$ gets a non-zero value, so it executes break and comes out of the loop.

So, process $C_2$ also executes printf("Hello!") once.

Total count

The processes that execute printf("Hello!") are:

$P_0,\ C_1,\ C_2,\ C_3$

So the total number of executions is $$4$$

content: Consider a CPU that has to execute two types of processes. The first type, Actuators (A), requires a CPU burst of 6 seconds. The second type, Controllers (C), requires a CPU burst of 8 seconds. A new process of type A arrives at time $t=10,20,30,40$, and 50 (in seconds). Similarly, a new process of type C arrives at time $t=11,22,33$, 44, and 55 (in seconds). The CPU scheduling policy is First Come First Serve (FCFS). The first process of type A starts running at $t=10$ seconds. The average waiting time (in seconds) for the 10 processes is $\_\_\_\_$ . (rounded off to one decimal place)
explanation:
$$ \text { Burst time } A=6 \mathrm{sec}, B=8 \mathrm{sec} $$

Process Arrival time Burst time CT TAT WT
A 10 6 16 6 0
C 11 8 24 13 5
A 20 6 30 10 4
C 22 8 38 16 8
A 30 6 44 14 8
C 33 8 52 19 11
A 40 6 58 18 12
C 44 8 66 22 14
A 50 6 72 22 16
C 55 8 80 25 17

$$ \text { CPU idle } $$

$$ \text { Average waiting time }=\frac{0+5+4+8+8+11+12+14+16+17}{10}=\frac{95}{10}=9.5 $$

content:
A computer has two processors, $M_1$ and $M_2$. Four processes $P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4$ with CPU bursts of $20,16,25$, and 10 milliseconds, respectively, arrive at the same time and these are the only processes in the system. The scheduler uses non-preemptive priority scheduling, with priorities decided as follows:

$\bullet$ $M_1$ uses priority of execution for the processes as, $P_1>P_3>P_2>P_4$, i.e., $P_1$ and $P_4$ have highest and lowest priorities, respectively.

$\bullet$ $M_2$ uses priority of execution for the processes as, $P_2>P_3>P_4>P_1$, i.e., $P_2$ and $P_1$ have highest and lowest priorities, respectively.

A process $P_i$ is scheduled to a processor $M_k$, if the processor is free and no other process $P_j$ is waiting with higher priority. At any given point of time, a process can be allocated to any one of the free processors without violating the execution priority rules. Ignore the context switch time. What will be the average waiting time of the processes in milliseconds?
explanation:
$\mathrm{P.No.}$ $\mathrm{A.T.}$ $\mathrm{B.T.}$ $\mathrm{C.T.}$ $\mathrm{T.A.T.}$ $\mathrm{W.T.}$
$P_1$ 0 20 20 20 0
$P_2$ 0 16 16 16 0
$P_3$ 0 25 45 45 20
$P_4$ 0 10 26 26 16

Average waiting time $=\frac{0+0+20+16}{4}=\frac{36}{4}=9$

content:
Consider a single processor system with four processes A, B, C, and D, represented as given below, where for each process the first value is its arrival time, and the second value is its CPU burst time.

A (0, 10), B (2, 6), C (4, 3), and D (6, 7).

Which one of the following options gives the average waiting times when preemptive Shortest Remaining Time First (SRTF) and Non-Preemptive Shortest Job First (NP-SJF) CPU scheduling algorithms are applied to the processes?
explanation:
To determine the average waiting times for both the Preemptive Shortest Remaining Time First (SRTF) and Non-Preemptive Shortest Job First (NP-SJF) CPU scheduling algorithms, we need to calculate the waiting times for each process, and then find the average of these waiting times.

First, let's calculate the waiting times using the SRTF (Preemptive) algorithm:

SRTF (Preemptive) Algorithm

1. At time 0, Process A starts execution (as it's the only process that arrived).

2. At time 2, Process B arrives, but A still has 8 units remaining, so A continues.

3. At time 4, Process C arrives. Now, the remaining times are:

  • A = 6 units
  • B = 6 units
  • C = 3 units

Hence, Process C starts executing.

4. At time 7, Process C completes. Process D arrives. Now, the remaining times are:

  • A = 6 units
  • B = 6 units
  • D = 7 units

Hence, Process B starts executing.

5. At time 13, Process B completes. Now, the remaining times are:

  • A = 6 units
  • D = 7 units

Hence, Process A resumes execution.

6. At time 19, Process A completes. Now, only Process D is left to execute.

7. At time 26, Process D completes.

Now, let's calculate the waiting times:

  • Waiting time for A = Finish time - Arrival time - Burst time = 19 - 0 - 10 = 9
  • Waiting time for B = Finish time - Arrival time - Burst time = 13 - 2 - 6 = 5
  • Waiting time for C = Finish time - Arrival time - Burst time = 7 - 4 - 3 = 0
  • Waiting time for D = Finish time - Arrival time - Burst time = 26 - 6 - 7 = 13

Average waiting time for SRTF = $$ \frac{9 + 5 + 0 + 13}{4} = 6.75 $$

NP-SJF (Non-Preemptive Shortest Job First) Algorithm

1. At time 0, Process A starts execution because it's the only process arrived.

2. At time 10, Process A finishes. Processes B, C, and D have arrived by this time. Process C has the shortest burst time, so it executes next.

3. At time 13, Process C finishes. Among B and D, Process B has the shortest burst time, so it executes next.

4. At time 19, Process B finishes, and finally Process D executes.

5. At time 26, Process D finishes.

Now, let's calculate the waiting times:

  • Waiting time for A = Finish time - Arrival time - Burst time = 10 - 0 - 10 = 0
  • Waiting time for B = Finish time - Arrival time - Burst time = 19 - 2 - 6 = 11
  • Waiting time for C = Finish time - Arrival time - Burst time = 13 - 4 - 3 = 6
  • Waiting time for D = Finish time - Arrival time - Burst time = 26 - 6 - 7 = 13

Average waiting time for NP-SJF = $$ \frac{0 + 11 + 6 + 13}{4} = 7.5 $$

Thus, the correct answer is:

Option B: SRTF = 6, NP-SJF = 7.5

content: Consider four processes P, Q, R and S scheduled on a CPU as per round robin algorithm with a time quantum of 4 units. The processes arrive in the order P, Q, R, S, all at time t = 0. There is exactly one context switch from S to Q, exactly one context switch from R to Q, and exactly two context switches from Q to R. There is no context switch from S to P. Switching to a ready process after the termination of another process is also considered a context switch. Which one of the following is NOT possible as CPU burst time (in time units) of these processes?
explanation:
The above grant chart satisfies all conditions given in the question the option (a), (b), (c) but not option (d).

So, answer is option (d).

content:
Consider the following set of processes, assumed to have arrived at time 0. Consider the CPU scheduling algorithms Shortest Job First (SJF) and Round Robin (RR). For RR, assume that the processes are scheduled in the order P1, P2, P3, P4.
If the time quantum for RR is 4 ms, then the absolute value of the difference between the average turnaround times (in ms) of SJF and RR (round off to 2 decimal places) is _____.
explanation:
Given :

SJF :

$$ T A T=(21-0)+(13-0)+(2-0)+(6-0) $$

Average $T A T=\frac{42}{4}=10.5$

RR :

$$ \begin{aligned} T A T= & (18-0)+(21-0)+(10-0)+(14-0) \\\\ & =18+21+10+14 \end{aligned} $$

Average $T A T=\frac{63}{4}=15.75$

Hence,

$$ \begin{aligned} & |S J F(T A T)-R R(T A T)|=|10.5-15.75| \\\\ & =5.25 \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct answer is 5.25 .

content:
Consider the following four processes with arrival times (in milliseconds) and their length of CPU bursts (in milliseconds) as shown below :

These processes are run on a single processor using preemptive Shortest Remaining Time First scheduling algorithm. If the average waiting time of the processes is 1 millisecond, then the value of Z is _____.
explanation:
$$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|} \hline \text { Process } & \text { Arrival Time } & \text { Burst Time } \\ \hline \text { P1 } & 0 & 3 \\ \hline \text { P2 } & 1 & 1 \\ \hline \text { P3 } & 3 & 3 \\ \hline \text { P4 } & 4 & \text { Z } \\ \hline \end{array} $$

Gantt chart till t = 4 :

Now, there are 2 processes which are waiting in ready queue $P_3$ and $P_4$

So, Case 1 : If $z<3$,

Hence, $z=1$ or 2 is only possible. After solving we will get $z=2$.

Case 2: If $z \geq 3$

Here, Average waiting time will not depend on $z$, hence $z>3$ is not possible.

Waiting Time = Turnaround Time - Burst Time

Hence, the correct answer is 2.

content: Consider a uniprocessor system executing three tasks T<sub>1</sub>, T<sub>2</sub> and T<sub>3</sub>, each of which is composed of an infinite sequence of jobs (or instances) which arrive periodically at intervals of 3, 7 and 20 milliseconds, respectively. The priority of each task is the inverse of its period, and the available tasks are scheduled in order of priority, with the highest priority task scheduled first. Each instance of T<sub>1</sub>, T<sub>2</sub> and T<sub>3</sub> requires an execution time of 1, 2 and 4 milliseconds, respectively. Given that all tasks initially arrive at the beginning of the 1<sup>st</sup> millisecond and task preemptions are allowed, the first instance of T<sub>3</sub> completes its execution at the end of _____________ milliseconds.

Three processes $$A, B$$ and $$C$$ each execute a loop of $$100$$ iterations. In each iteration of the loop, a process performs a single computation that requires $${t_c}\,\,CPU$$ milliseconds and then initiates a single $${\rm I}/O$$ operation that lasts for $${t_{io}}$$ milliseconds. It is assumed that the computer where the processes execute has sufficient number of $${\rm I}/O$$ devices and the $$OS$$ of the computer assigns different $${\rm I}/O$$ devices to each process. Also, the scheduling overhead of the $$OS$$ is negligible. The processes have the following characteristics:
Process id tc tio
A 100 ms 500 ms
B 350 ms 500 ms
C 200 ms 500 ms

The processes $$A, B,$$ and $$C$$ are started at times $$0, 5$$ and $$10$$ milliseconds respectively, in a pure time sharing system (round robin scheduling) that uses a time slice of $$50$$ milliseconds. The time in milliseconds at which process $$C$$ would complete its first $${\rm I}/O$$ operation is __________.

Consider the following set of processes that need to be scheduled on a single $$CPU.$$ All the times are given in milliseconds.
Process Name Arrival Time Execution Time
A 0 6
B 3 2
C 5 4
D 7 6
E 10 3

Using the $$shortest$$ $$remaining$$ $$time$$ $$first$$ scheduling algorithm, the average process turnaround time (in $$msec$$) is _______.

An operating system uses $$shortest$$ $$remaining$$ $$time$$ $$first$$ scheduling algorithm for pre-emptive scheduling of processes. Consider the following set of processes with their arrival times and $$CPU$$ burst times (in milliseconds):
Process Arrival Time Burst Time
P1 0 12
P2 2 4
P3 3 6
P4 8 5

The average waiting time (in milliseconds) of the processes is ________.
explanation:
*Completion Time is found from Gantt Chart. To find completion time go from right to left in the Gantt Chart and see last time a process executed. That will be completion time of that process.

*Turnaroud Time = Completion Time - Arrival Time

*Waiting Time = Turnarund Time - Burst Time

content:

(i) each process is in one of the five states: created, ready, running, blocked (i.e. sleep or wait), or terminated, and
(ii) only non-preemptive scheduling is used by the $$OS.$$ Label the transitions appropriately.

Which combination of the following features will suffice to characterize an $$OS$$ as a multi-programmed $$OS?$$
$$(a).$$ More than one program may be loaded into main memory at the same time for execution.
$$(b).$$ If a program waits for certain events such as $$I/O$$, another program is immediately scheduled for execution.
$$(c).$$ If the execution of a program terminates, another program is immediately scheduled for execution.

A multi-user, multi-processing operating system cannot be implemented on hardware that does not support:

options.content: At least two modes of CPU execution (privileged and non-privileged)

Swapping out the memory image of process A to the disk.

content: Four jobs are waiting to be run. Their expected run times are $$6, 3, 5$$ and $$x$$. $${\rm I}$$n what order should they be run to minimize the average response time?
